segments.text: Now we come to the chapter of the 2s, we have finished with the chapter of the 1s,
and we turn into a chapter of the 2s, adukani pata.
The Buddha said monks, these two struggles are hard to undergo in the world.
What to?
The struggle of householders will live at home to provide clothing, food, lodging, medicines for the sick
and provision of necessities.
And the struggle of those who have gone forth from home to the homeless,
to renounce all basis of rebirth.
These are the two struggles.
Of these two monks, the latter is the more important.
Therefore I say unto you,
thus must you train yourselves.
We will undertake the struggle to renounce all substrates of rebirth,
or all basis of rebirth.
That is how you must train yourself monks.
So the Buddha says, that's the end of the sutra.
The Buddha is saying that the struggle of householders is to provide the necessities for the home.
And the struggle of monks is to renounce the supports of the basis of rebirth.
And the struggle of monks is the more important.
Sometimes we find a lot of people, they go through life, struggling.
One thing after another, many young, lot of people, they struggle to get a good education.
And after that, they want to get a good job.
After that, they want a good marriage partner.
And after that, they want children.
One thing after another, they want a car and a house.
And grandchildren later, etc.
And then at the end of it all, a lot of people, when they are about to die.
They are very confused, they are very disappointed.
And then they wonder what life is all about.
All the things that they wanted, the struggle for, most of it they have attained.
And suddenly they find that they have to lose all of it.
So we keep on coming back again and again to do this kind of struggle.
But the Buddha said the struggle to end the cycle of rebirth is more important.
And then the next 2.1.3 monks, there are these two things that burn.
But to hear in a certain one, has done immoral acts of body.
He has done immoral acts in speech and thought.
He has omitted moral acts in body, speech and thought.
His burned or seared with remorse at the thought.
I have done wrong in body, speech and thought.
I have left and done the good deeds in body, speech and thought.
And he burns at the thought of it.
These monks are the two things that burn.
So the Buddha says, if we have done immoral acts in moral acts,
immoral karma in body, speech and mind,
all we have omitted to do moral acts,
immoral karma, wholesome karma in body, speech and thought,
then our conscience is prick.
So you see, sometimes it's not only that we do something immoral.
Even if we fail to do something that we ought to do later,
it will burn our conscience and we get prick by it.
That's why we have to be careful.
What are the immoral acts?
For example, immoral acts of body are my be killing,
committing adultery or stealing.
And immoral acts of speech are my be lying, slandering,
caring tales so that people grow.
I said, and then immoral act of thought might be having hatred
for somebody, etc.
Now the next two times, two point one, point five,
the Buddha said, two things monks have I realized,
to be discontented in good states and not to shrink back from the struggle.
Without shrinking back monks, I struggle on thus.
Clearly, would I have skin and sinews and bones with her,
and my body's flesh and blood dry up?
If only I may hold out until I win what may be one by human strength,
by human energy by human striving.
By my earnest endeavour monks, I won enlightenment,
I won the unreliable freedom from the born.
And you two monks, do not you decline the fight,
but struggle on saying to yourselves,
gladly would I have my skin and sinews and bones with her,
and my body's flesh and blood dry up?
If only I may hold out until I win what may be one by human strength,
by human energy by human striving.
Then you two monks in no long time,
shall win that goal for which the clansmen,
rightly leave home for the homeless life,
even that unrivaled goal of righteous living,
realizing it for yourselves even in this very life,
and having reached it you shall abide therein.
Therefore I see unto you monks,
thus must you train yourselves,
we will not decline the fight.
But will struggle on with this thought,
let skin and sinews and bones with her, etc.
It is how you must train yourselves monks,
let's see end of the sutra.
There's a sutra in the Majima Nikaya,
I think number 12 where the Buddha described the struggle
that he underwent the six years,
the various types of suffering he underwent,
and he was quite moving to read the way the Buddha struggled,
and the Buddha said,
that nobody in the past could have suffered more than him
on the spiritual path,
and in the future the Buddha said,
nobody also could possibly suffer more than him on the spiritual path,
at the very most it could only equal the amount of suffering he went through.
So you see that our teacher is somebody who exerted himself
and the Buddha, that's why also encourage us to put in a lot of effort.
And how much effort we put in,
we determine how much we get out of the spiritual path,
and basically it is concerned with letting go,
letting go the spiritual path is concerned with letting go of the ego,
letting go without likes and dislikes.
Our opinions and etc.
And anything to do with the ego,
that's why it's very important to always observe our mind,
the sin is a meditation,
should always be observing his mind,
see the ego working.
The next Buddha is 2.1.6,
Buddha said,
man, there are these two things,
what to,
looking with satisfaction on things which are
factors that bind to rebirth,
and looking with disgusts there on.
Amongst, he who dwells looking with satisfaction on things
that bind like factors,
abundance, not greed,
abundance, not hatred, abundance, not delusion.
He who abandons not these,
is not release from,
rebirth from all age, from sickness, from death,
and sorrow, and grief, whole limitation, and despair.
He is not released from Dukha,
here I say,
but monks, he who dwells looking with disgust on things,
which are factors that bind,
abundance, greed, hatred, and delusion,
abandoning these, he's released from all age, and decay, etc.
He's released from Dukha, and decay.
These monks are the two things.
See, I know the Sutta.
So the Buddha is saying that there are certain things,
which are factors,
attachments, attachments,
to our relatives,
attachments, to our property,
our wealth, these are the two greatest attachments.
The Dhamma Padha, the Buddha said,
the chains, the eye and chains,
that bind a person,
and nothing compared to the bonds that are created
from family ties, probably ties,
ties of our clothes, family ties of our property, etc.
Those are real bonds.
So a person who is not learned in the Dhamma,
and those things that are factors to that person,
that means those things are things that bind him
to the wheel of Samsara.
He looks with satisfaction on them.
He's very happy and satisfied,
and he's got a big house,
a lot of money in the bank.
He's got very beautiful children,
very loving husband, very loving wife, etc.
Those things, and that two, a worldly person,
are things that give him joy and happiness,
are those very things that bind us to Samsara.
That is why it is very difficult to walk the path,
the spiritual path, most people.
They want to enjoy those things,
because it's very normal for worldly people,
for normal human beings,
to enjoy things like that,
having a loving husband, loving wife,
loving children, etc.
And they get a lot of joy and satisfaction,
enjoying good music,
a nice TV set,
like a straw TV,
and those are the very things that
bring us back to rebirth.
So the Buddha says,
if we have the wisdom of the Dhamma,
from listening to the Dhamma,
then we know that these are the things that bring us back to rebirth.
That's why the Buddha says in the Dhamma Pada,
that the worldly path is in one direction,
the path of Nirvana is in the opposite direction.
Some people, they think they want to get the best of both worlds.
They want to put one foot in one boat and one foot in the other boat.
And it's very difficult to do that.
If you make any progress after some time,
you realize that you really want to cultivate and you have to renounce.
If you don't renounce,
it's very difficult to practice.
The Buddha says,
these two bright states protect the world,
what two sense of shame and fear of blame.
Here is Othapal.
Which probably can be better translated as conscience and remorse.
And the Buddha continues,
if these two states did not protect the world,
then there would be seen no mother or mother's sister.
No uncles, wife, not teachers, wife,
no wife of honorable men.
But the world would come to confusion.
Promiscuity such as exists among goats and sheep,
fowls and swine, dogs and jackals.
But among, since these two bright states do protect the world,
therefore there are seen mothers and mothers, sisters, uncles and uncles,
why these are why they are set right, et cetera.
As the end of the suta.
So the Buddha is saying that because we have a sense of conscience and remorse,
conscience meaning like the fear to do something wrong,
a remorse meaning that,
to creed to fear remorse over some wrong that he had done.
People without a sense of remorse, they do something wrong
and they don't feel the conscience breaking them.
Maybe the conscience does break them,
but they just keep on doing the wrong things until,
how can they break them?
That's not paying them anymore.
So a person with remorse and conscience,
he would not do things that animals do.
The Buddha is saying, talking here about promiscuity.
You see animals, they don't recognize mother and son or daughter,
et cetera.
They just are very promiscuous.
So human beings don't do that,
but sometimes you find human,
some human beings do that.
Especially nowadays, you read in the papers,
there's a lot of cases of incest, et cetera.
So if we behave like animals,
then it's very likely next time we come back to repeat.
If we behave like they were,
then it's very likely that we will have re-birth in the devaram.
Two point two point five.
The Buddha said,
monks, if in any dispute,
the offending monk and the reproving monk,
do not practice strict self-examination,
it may be expected that it will continue to protract it,
bitter, contentious, strife,
and the monks will be unable to live at ease.
And how do the two parties practice strict self-examination?
Here in the offending monk reflects us.
I have fallen into somebody's ear fence.
Now, if we behave like this,
we can't do that.
If we behave like this,
we can't do that.
But when, in any dispute,
both the offending monk and the reproving monk,
do practice strict self-examination,
it may be expected that it will not continue to protract it,
now,
the other monks saw some particular occasion of bodily offence
into which I had fallen.
Had I not so offended,
he could not have seen it.
Since I saw offended, he saw it.
Seeing it, he was annoyed.
Being annoyed there, he gave utterance to his annoyance.
Thus, rebuked by him.
In his annoyance, I also was annoyed,
and told others of my annoyance.
So, hearing my fall overcame me,
as in the case of one who has to pay duty on his goods.
That is how the offending monk practices strict self-examination.
And how monks does the reproving monk do likewise.
Hearing the reproving monk does reflex.
This monk has fallen into somebody's ear fence.
Indeed, I saw this monk so falling into a fence.
Had he not done so,
I should not have seen him so doing.
As he did so, I saw him so doing.
At the sight of this, I was displaced there.
Being displaced, I expressed my displeasure to this monk.
Thus annoyed by my expression of annoyance.
This monk told others of his annoyance.
So, hearing my fall overcame me,
as in the case of one who has to pay duty on his goods.
That is how the reproving monk practices strict self-examination.
Now, if both the offending and reproving monk do not practice strict self-examination,
it may be expected that it will induce to protracted bitter contentious strife.
And the monks will be unable to live at ease.
But if, on the contrary, they do practice strict self-examination,
it may be expected that the opposite will happen.
As the end of the suta.
This suta is more for monks,
but it is equally applicable to lay people.
Sometimes you find two parties quarreling.
And the Buddha is saying that if we are involved in any quarrel or in any ill-feeling with somebody else,
we have to examine ourselves and be frank and see our thoughts.
And if we practice self-examination and admit our thoughts,
then we can apologize or at least even if we don't apologize.
We don't make matters worse by prolonging the dispute now, whatever.
The Brahmin Janusoni came to visit the exalted one,
and after being received respect,
etc.
sat down at one side,
so seated he said to the exalted one,
pray, master gotama,
what is the reason, what is the cause,
why some beings here in this world when body breaks up after death,
are reborn in the waste, the wolf who planes the downfall in hell.
The Buddha said, owing to commission an omission Brahmin,
and then Janusoni asked again,
but pray, master gotama, why are they reborn when body breaks up after death,
in the happy lot in the heaven world,
again the Buddha said, owing to commission an omission Brahmin,
and Janusoni said,
I do not understand the detailed meaning of one,
has been concisely stated by the word he gotama,
but not explained in detail,
it will be well for me the word he gotama,
would teach me damma in such a way that I might understand his meaning in detail,
and the Buddha said, then Brahmin, do you listen,
give careful attention and I will speak.
Very good sir replied, the Brahmin Janusoni to the exalted one,
that's all that one said,
now in this connection Brahmin, certain beings have committed,
immoral acts of body speech and thought,
they have omitted moral acts of body speech and thought,
thus Brahmin, it is owing to commission an omission
that beings are reborn in the waste,
the wolf who plains the downfall in hell,
again Brahmin, certain beings have committed moral acts of body speech and thought,
they have omitted immoral acts of body speech and thought,
thus again it is owing to commission an omission
that beings after death are reborn in the heaven world,
excellent master gotama, excellent master gotama,
etc etc.
and then Brahmin took refuge in the Buddha.
So here this Buddha again is about karma,
the Buddha again is saying that karma has two parts of it,
that doing of something and neglecting to do something.
So just like I explained just now,
both we have to look after,
just like for example, if we create good karma towards our parents,
that is good karma,
but because our parents have done a lot for us,
so if we neglect to look after our parents,
and then again it is very bad karma.
Now we will come to another sutra, which is a previous sutra,
which I missed out the last time,
that is 2.3.9, the Buddha said,
monks, it is because I observe these two results,
therein that I am given to dwelling in lonely spots,
in solitary lodging in the forest,
what to observe in my own pleasant way of living in this very life,
and feeling compassion for future generations,
these are the two results as the end of the sutra.
Here the Buddha is saying that he likes to live in secluded places,
live alone in the forest or caves etc.
For two reasons, one is his old pleasant way of living,
that means he has a pleasant abiding for himself,
because when a monk stays alone,
there is nobody to disturb him,
and he is very free to do what he likes,
and especially if a monk is good in meditation,
he can dispend his time in meditation,
which keeps him a lot of bliss,
and a lot of advantage in the spiritual life,
because the Buddha said that the cultivation of the Janas
will give you four advantages,
a cultivation of the mind, a cultivation of somebody,
a concentration,
the before advantages namely the fruits of Arya Buddha,
Sotapana, Sakatakami, Anakami and Arahan Buddha,
this is mentioned in the Dikana Kaya.
He also said somewhere in the sutra or in the Vinaya,
he says, when he lives alone in the forest,
then he can if he was so free,
and that even when he wants to answer the calls of nature,
he doesn't have to look around to see whether anybody is around,
and the second reason is feeling compassion for future generations.
This is because of compassion for future generations,
he is showing the way for monks,
and always encouraging monks to stay in secret places
to cultivate themselves,
and he also always encouraged monks to practice alone,
stay in secret places and practice alone,
but of course not all monks can do it,
unless you come to that stage where you can live alone,
if you go and try to live alone,
when you're not ready,
it can have a negative impact instead of a positive.
The other thing I'd like to mention here concerning this sutra
is in the Mahayana teachings,
there is a little bit of contradiction also concerning this one,
because in the Mahayana Purisatoa precepts,
one of the minor Purisatoa precepts,
is that a Purisatoa is not allowed of forbidden to stay alone
in the quiet place around here.
The secluded place is not allowed according to the Purisatoa precepts,
and Purisatoa precepts to stay alone.
Whereas here, even the Buddha is already enlightened,
and yet he likes to stay alone,
and he also encourages his disciples who are not enlightened yet,
to also dwell alone to cultivate themselves.
The other person whose famous was staying alone is Mahaka Sapa,
the very ecstatic monk,
the disciple of the Buddha.
